title: The Precision-Recall Curve
concept: The Precision-Recall Curve shows the tradeoff between precision and recall across different decision thresholds, helping businesses balance false alarms against missed detections.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does the F1-score measure by combining precision and recall?

options.text: The harmonic mean of precision and recall, balancing both false positives and false negatives.
The arithmetic mean, highlighting only false positives.
The ratio of true positives to the total number of predicted positives.

exercises.explain: Correct! The F1-score uses the harmonic mean to give a balanced picture of both precision and recall. Now, let's look at how we navigate the tradeoff between them.

cards.title: The security system slider
cards.body: Imagine adjusting a home security alarm. If you make it too sensitive, it triggers on passing cats (high recall, low precision). If you make it too strict, it only triggers for actual burglars but might miss a quiet break-in (high precision, low recall).

cards.title: The threshold tradeoff
cards.body: A classification model outputs a probability (like 72%). We use a decision threshold to turn that number into a 'Yes' or 'No'. Shifting this threshold slider is what trade-offs precision against recall.

cards.title: The fishing net
cards.body: Imagine fishing. Using a massive, fine-mesh net catches every single fish (100% recall) but also scoops up leaves, trash, and boots (low precision). Using a small hand spear catches only high-quality fish (high precision) but misses most of the school (low recall).

cards.title: Choosing for business need
cards.body: In cancer screening, we want to catch every case, so we prefer high recall (even if we get false alarms). In spam filtering, we don't want to delete important emails, so we demand high precision (even if a few spam emails get through).

cards.title: Can we get 100% of both?
cards.body: It is a common myth that a model can always achieve both perfect precision and perfect recall. In the real world, increasing one almost always decreases the other because boundaries between classes are rarely perfectly clean.

cards.title: The PR curve visualization
cards.body: The Precision-Recall Curve plots these two metrics at every possible threshold.

left.text:
Few alerts, but highly accurate.
High Precision, Low Recall.

right.text:
Many alerts, catching everything.
Low Precision, High Recall.

exercises.prompt: If you lower the decision threshold of a spam filter from 0.5 to 0.2, what will happen to its recall?

options.text: Recall will increase because it will catch more spam emails overall.
Recall will decrease because it will catch fewer spam emails.
Recall will stay the same because the dataset hasn't changed.

exercises.explain: Lowering the threshold makes the model more eager to predict 'spam' (positive), which catches more of the actual spam (higher recall) but increases false alarms.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Shifting the decision threshold is done during model evaluation/deployment, without retargeting or retraining the model itself.
exercises.explain: True! You don't need to retrain the model to change the threshold. You just adjust the threshold value used to convert predicted probabilities into labels.

exercises.sentence: If a business cannot tolerate false alarms, they should select a threshold that maximizes ___.
exercises.explain: Precision measures the percentage of alerts that are actually correct. High precision minimizes false alarms (false positives).

exercises.prompt: Which scenario correctly demands a high-recall threshold over high-precision?

options.text: An airport luggage scanner trying to detect explosives, where missing a threat is catastrophic.
A push notification service recommending matching clothes, where annoying the user is a bad experience.
An automated email archiver that stores receipts, where archiving a regular message by accident must be avoided.

exercises.explain: When missing a positive case (a threat) has extreme consequences, we prioritize recall to catch every possible threat, even if it means more manual inspection (false alarms).

exercises.prompt: You are deploying a transaction fraud detector for a bank. The bank has a manual review team that is small and easily overwhelmed by false alerts. However, they also face a $100 penalty for every fraudulent transaction that goes undetected. Which threshold strategy is best?

options.text: Set a moderate-to-high threshold to keep precision high and prevent review team burnout, while monitoring the rate of missed fraud.
Set the threshold to 0.0 to flag every transaction as fraud and ensure 100% recall.
Set the threshold to 1.0 to disable all alerts and save manual review time completely.
Set a random threshold daily to keep scammers guessing.

exercises.explain: By using a moderate-to-high threshold, you protect the small review team from alert fatigue. Monitoring missed fraud ensures you can adjust the threshold if the penalties start costing too much. 🎉
